pending_diagnosis: DIAGNOSIS: Methanol Poisoning (Delayed Presentation) THE SMOKING GUN: Severe Anion Gap Metabolic Acidosis (AGMA) with a Normal Lactic Acid Level and the symmetrical, bilateral basal ganglia necrosis seen on MRI. PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: Methanol itself is non-toxic, but it is metabolized primarily in the liver (via Alcohol Dehydrogenase) first to formaldehyde, and then rapidly (via Aldehyde Dehydrogenase) to formic acid. Formic acid is highly toxic. Unlike ethanol poisoning, methanol poisoning involves a delay in symptom onset (12-72 hours) because the patient must metabolize the parent compound into the toxic metabolite (formic acid). 1. Metabolic Acidosis: The profound AGMA is due almost entirely to the accumulation of formic acid. The absence of lactic acidosis rules out typical shock states and points strongly toward a primary toxic alcohol ingestion. 2. Neurotoxicity: Formic acid is a specific mitochondrial toxin, inhibiting cytochrome c oxidase. It selectively damages areas of high metabolic demand, most notably the putamen, globus pallidus, and optic nerves, leading to the characteristic bilateral basal ganglia necrosis on MRI and potential permanent blindness (which Mr. H.R. likely would have suffered if he survived). TREATMENT & OUTCOME: The diagnosis was confirmed by detecting a grossly elevated serum methanol level (Methanol screening was only performed after the critical metabolic shift was recognized). Treatment involved immediate administration of Fomepizole (or high-dose Ethanol) to competitively inhibit Alcohol Dehydrogenase, preventing further toxic metabolite production, and urgent Hemodialysis to rapidly clear the formic acid and methanol. Despite aggressive intervention, Mr. H.R. suffered irreversible brain death due to extensive cytotoxic edema and profound cerebral metabolic collapse.
